rankings.name: Scottie Scheffler
rankings.rationale: The most complete player in the world remains the man to beat at Augusta. Scheffler's iron play is borderline unfair on this course, and his two green jackets in three years tell you everything. His strokes gained approach numbers are historic, ranking first on the PGA Tour for three consecutive seasons. The field is chasing a ghost who seems to elevate his game at the precise moments that matter most. With three wins already in 2026, he arrives at Magnolia Lane in the form of his life and with the quiet confidence of a man who knows exactly how to navigate every inch of Augusta National.

rankings.name: Jon Rahm
rankings.rationale: Rahm's move to LIV hasn't dulled his edge at the majors -- his 2023 green jacket proved that. His combination of power and touch around Augusta's greens makes him a perpetual top-5 favorite. The limited reps against top fields are the only question mark, and even that feels like a minor quibble. Rahm's ball-striking remains elite by any measure, and his competitive fire burns as hot as anyone's in the field. The Spaniard's Seve-like flair for the dramatic, combined with modern power and precision, creates a player who is perfectly suited to Augusta's demands. With a T4 and T8 in his last two Masters starts, he has been remarkably consistent despite the LIV transition.

rankings.name: Bryson DeChambeau
rankings.rationale: The Scientist has turned Augusta into a par-68 course with his driver, but the short game has always been his Achilles heel on these slick greens. Back-to-back US Open titles give him undeniable major pedigree. If the wedges cooperate, look out. His length advantage on the par-5s is among the most significant in the field, and his analytical approach to course management has only sharpened since joining LIV. The wildcard factor is real -- when everything clicks, he can shoot numbers nobody else in the field is capable of.

rankings.name: Rory McIlroy
rankings.rationale: The defending champion returns to Magnolia Lane having slain the dragon that haunted him for a decade. That playoff victory over Justin Rose last April completed the career Grand Slam and lifted an enormous psychological burden. The question now: does the monkey off his back unleash a new Rory, or was last year the summit? His 2026 form has been solid but not spectacular, with one win and four top-10s suggesting he's managing his schedule carefully for a title defense. The raw talent remains breathtaking -- nobody hits a golf ball quite like McIlroy when he's firing -- and defending at Augusta with the Grand Slam complete could be the ultimate pressure-free environment.

rankings.name: Xander Schauffele
rankings.rationale: Since breaking through at the 2024 PGA and Open, Schauffele has played with a different gear. Augusta has been his white whale -- five top-10s without a jacket. The putter is finally matching the ball-striking, and that's terrifying for everyone else. His all-around game has no glaring weakness, making him the most consistent contender in the field. With two majors now on his resume, the mental barrier that once held him back at Augusta has been shattered, and he arrives in 2026 as a complete player who knows he belongs at the top of any leaderboard.

rankings.name: Ludvig Åberg
rankings.rationale: The Swede nearly won the thing as a Masters rookie in 2024 and backed it up with another top-5 last year. At 26, Aberg has the length, the temperament, and the trajectory of a future champion. It's not a matter of if, but when -- and Augusta feels like where. His ball-striking numbers off the tee are the best in the field, combining elite distance with accuracy that belies his power. The Swedish sensation has handled the pressure of being golf's next big thing with remarkable composure, displaying a stoic Nordic calm that is perfectly suited to the cauldron of Masters Sunday. With two strong Augusta starts under his belt, the course knowledge gap that usually holds back young players has been significantly narrowed.

rankings.name: Cameron Young
rankings.rationale: Young's prodigious length turns Augusta's par-5s into birdie machines, and the all-around game has matured considerably. He's overdue for a major moment.

rankings.name: Tommy Fleetwood
rankings.rationale: Fleetwood's silky ball-striking is a perfect fit for Augusta, and the Englishman is riding the confidence of his breakthrough PGA Tour wins. He's been knocking on the door at majors for years, and his steady improvement at Augusta -- from T20 to T5 to T7 -- suggests a player learning the course's secrets. At 35, the clock is ticking but the game has never been sharper, and his Ryder Cup pedigree proves he can handle the biggest stages.

rankings.name: Hideki Matsuyama
rankings.rationale: The 2021 champion knows every blade of grass at Augusta National. When Matsuyama gets hot with the irons, he's as dangerous as anyone in the field. His ball-striking can carry an average putting week -- and sometimes that's all you need on these greens. Twelve Masters appearances have given him an encyclopedic knowledge of the course that few can match, and his iron play remains among the very best in the world. The Japanese star is a quiet assassin who has proven he can handle the biggest stage in golf.

rankings.name: Collin Morikawa
rankings.rationale: Morikawa's iron play is tailor-made for Augusta's second-shot course, and the improved putting that carried him through 2025 has held. Two majors at 29 with none at Augusta feels like an oversight that's about to be corrected. His ball-striking precision ranks among the best on Tour, and his ability to control trajectory in all conditions makes him a nightmare for the field on Augusta's undulating greens. The question has always been the putter, but the flatstick has quietly become a neutral-to-positive weapon in 2026.

rankings.name: Justin Rose
rankings.rationale: Rose came agonizingly close to the green jacket in last year's playoff loss to McIlroy, and the heartbreak at 44 was palpable. At 45, this might be his last realistic crack -- and the motivation of unfinished business is powerful.

rankings.name: Robert MacIntyre
rankings.rationale: The fiery Scotsman has blossomed on the PGA Tour and brings a fearless, go-for-broke mentality that either produces magic or mayhem at Augusta. His left-handed swing is tailor-made for the dogleg-right holes.

rankings.name: Min Woo Lee
rankings.rationale: The younger Lee sibling has electric talent and length to burn. His breakout PGA Tour campaign makes him a live dark horse at Augusta.

rankings.name: Jordan Spieth
rankings.rationale: The 2015 champion hasn't been the same player in years, but Augusta is his spiritual home. Spieth sees shots here that others can't imagine, and his creative genius around these greens is timeless. If the putter gets hot for 72 holes, stranger things have happened.

rankings.name: Brooks Koepka
rankings.rationale: Koepka's major record speaks for itself -- five wins and a near-miss at the 2023 Masters. The body has taken a beating, but he has an uncanny ability to peak for the biggest stages. His LIV form has been inconsistent, but dismissing a five-time major champion at a course where he finished runner-up is a fool's errand. Write him off at your own peril.

rankings.name: Patrick Reed
rankings.rationale: Captain America's 2018 triumph feels like a lifetime ago. Reed's game has declined sharply, and his Masters week is now more about the lifetime exemption than genuine contention.

rankings.name: Viktor Hovland
rankings.rationale: Hovland's short game transformation is complete, and the Norwegian is playing the best golf of his life in 2026. Augusta's green complexes used to expose him -- now they're a weapon. A maiden major feels imminent. His two wins and five top-10s in 2026 demonstrate a player who has eliminated the weaknesses that held him back, and his ball-striking has always been elite enough to contend at Augusta. The only question left is whether he can handle the pressure of contending for a first major on Sunday at Augusta National.

rankings.name: Si Woo Kim
rankings.rationale: Si Woo Kim's enigmatic talent can produce both 64s and 84s. When he's on, he can beat anyone; when he's off, well...

rankings.name: Akshay Bhatia
rankings.rationale: Bhatia's lefty creativity and explosive talent make him a fascinating wildcard. At 24, his best golf is well ahead of him, and Augusta rewards shotmakers.

rankings.name: Russell Henley
rankings.rationale: Henley is the quintessential under-the-radar contender -- methodical, precise, and lethal with mid-irons. Augusta rewards his brand of disciplined golf.

rankings.name: Justin Thomas
rankings.rationale: JT's resurgence in 2026 has quieted the doubters. The two-time PGA champion has always had the shot-making chops for Augusta -- his up-and-down game with the wedges here is elite. A green jacket would cap a redemption arc for the ages.

rankings.name: Adam Scott
rankings.rationale: The 2013 champion still has the prettiest swing in golf at 45, and his Augusta knowledge is encyclopedic. Father Time is undefeated, but Scott competes with grace.

rankings.name: Jake Knapp
rankings.rationale: Knapp's prodigious length makes Augusta's par-5s reachable with a wedge in hand. If the rest of the game catches up to the driver, watch out.

rankings.name: Shane Lowry
rankings.rationale: The Irishman's game ages like fine wine. Lowry's touch and imagination around the greens have elevated his Masters results in recent years, and his emotional intelligence in major pressure situations is elite. Don't sleep on him.

rankings.name: Patrick Cantlay
rankings.rationale: Cantlay's game is a metronome -- boring in the best possible way. That T3 last year showed he has the patience and putting touch for Augusta. The question remains whether his conservative approach can generate enough birdies on the par-5s to keep up with the bombers. His 2025 Masters performance was a masterclass in course management and patience, posting -10 without ever appearing to force anything. At 34, he is running out of runway, but the game has never been more suited to Augusta's demands.

rankings.name: Sam Burns
rankings.rationale: Burns has steadily climbed the world rankings with a well-rounded game and clutch putting. His Augusta pedigree is building, and a breakthrough week feels close.

rankings.name: Jason Day
rankings.rationale: Day's body has betrayed his immense talent for years, but Augusta's par-5s and his creative short game keep him in the conversation. The back is always a concern.

rankings.name: Nicolai Højgaard
rankings.rationale: The Danish twin has serious talent and the fearlessness of youth. Gaining valuable Augusta experience with each trip down Magnolia Lane.

rankings.name: Sepp Straka
rankings.rationale: The Austrian has quietly become one of the more reliable players on Tour. His length and solid approach play make him a competent Augusta contender.

rankings.name: Corey Conners
rankings.rationale: One of the purest ball-strikers on Tour, Conners' approach play is perfectly suited for Augusta's demanding second shots. If the putter ever shows up for four days, watch out.

rankings.name: Tyrrell Hatton
rankings.rationale: Hatton's fiery temperament and crisp ball-striking make him compelling theater at Augusta. The move to LIV hasn't dampened his major ambitions.

A recent LIV win has his confidence trending upward, and his calm temperament under pressure makes him dangerous whenever he tees it up in a major.

rankings.rationale: Clark's raw power and improving course management have made him a legitimate threat at Augusta. The 2023 US Open winner has learned how to navigate major pressure, and his upward trajectory at the Masters -- MC to T14 to T8 in three consecutive years -- suggests a big week is coming. His driving distance gives him a structural edge on the par 5s, and at 32, he is entering his prime with the game and the confidence to contend.

rankings.rationale: Michael Kim is competing in the 2026 Masters Tournament.

simulator.description: Monte Carlo simulation engine using strokes gained and course history data
simulator.methodology: Runs 10,000 tournament simulations using player strokes gained profiles, Augusta National course fit metrics, and recent form. Win/place probabilities are calibrated against real DraftKings odds.

scout.description: Qualitative scouting model weighing momentum, experience, and intangibles
scout.methodology: Evaluates players across five dimensions: recent momentum, Augusta experience, major championship clutch performance, weather adaptability, and intangible factors. Scores are weighted and combined with DraftKings implied probabilities.

analyst.description: Statistical model combining advanced metrics with betting market efficiency
analyst.methodology: Blends strokes gained approach, par-5 scoring, scrambling, and historical Augusta performance with market-implied probabilities from DraftKings to identify value and project outcomes.
analyst.narrative: The 2026 Masters field features 91 competitors at Augusta National. Market odds from DraftKings serve as the foundation for probability estimates, with analytical adjustments based on course-specific performance metrics.

rankings.name: Wyndham Clark
rankings.rationale: Clark's raw power and improving course management have made him a legitimate threat at Augusta. The 2023 US Open winner has learned how to navigate major pressure, and his upward trajectory at the Masters -- MC to T14 to T8 in three consecutive years -- suggests a big week is coming. His driving distance gives him a structural edge on the par 5s, and at 32, he is entering his prime with the game and the confidence to contend.

rankings.name: Casey Jarvis
rankings.rationale: Casey Jarvis is competing in the 2026 Masters Tournament.

rankings.name: Sergio García
rankings.rationale: The 2017 champion returns to the scene of his greatest triumph, but the competitive edge has faded considerably on the LIV circuit. A sentimental favorite more than a genuine contender.

rankings.name: Aaron Rai
rankings.rationale: Rai's precision approach play and quiet consistency have earned him a spot among the game's best. A thinking man's golfer at a thinking man's course.

rankings.name: Dustin Johnson
rankings.rationale: The 2020 champion's best golf is behind him, but DJ's natural talent and Augusta knowledge mean he can never be fully dismissed. That said, the competitive edge has clearly dulled on the LIV circuit.

rankings.name: Michael Kim
rankings.rationale: Michael Kim is competing in the 2026 Masters Tournament.
